notable.notable_blurb: Explorer and author; son of Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: He died by his own hand in Alaska
narrative_blocks.body: Kermit joined his father on the African safari and the near-fatal Amazon expedition, and fought in both World Wars, first in the British army. Long burdened by alcoholism and depression, he died by suicide at an army post in Alaska in 1943.

narrative_blocks.header: He helped found a transatlantic steamship line
narrative_blocks.body: An explorer and writer, Roosevelt co-founded the Roosevelt Steamship Company, which became part of the United States Lines. He published books on hunting and travel.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept his father alive on the Amazon
narrative_blocks.body: On the 1914 expedition down Brazil's River of Doubt, Kermit rationed his own quinine to his malarial father and was credited with saving the former President's life. He nearly died of the fever himself.

narrative_blocks.header: He won the Military Cross in Mesopotamia
narrative_blocks.body: In 1917 he took an honorary captaincy in the British Army and fought under General Maude in the Mesopotamian campaign, where he earned the Military Cross. He crossed to the American artillery in France for the war's last offensive.

narrative_blocks.header: He tore through Harvard in record time
narrative_blocks.body: A voracious reader, he compressed the four-year Harvard course into about two and a half years, breaking off after his freshman year to join his father's African safari. He took his degree in 1912.

cross_connections.display_label: re-ran the River of Doubt eight decades later

bio.bio_blurb: Explorer, soldier, son of Theodore Roosevelt

military_service.unit: 14th Light Armored Motor Battery, Machine Gun Corps
military_service.notes: Served under General Maude in the Mesopotamian campaign, 1917-1918.
Transferred to the American Expeditionary Force in France, 1918; Meuse-Argonne Offensive.
Intelligence officer; helped raise a territorial militia of Alaska Natives.

narrative_blocks.header: She built a school for disabled children
narrative_blocks.body: In memory of her mother, Belle donated family land to create the Belle Willard School, the first Fairfax County school built for special-needs children. It opened in 1957 with wheelchair ramps and therapy rooms.

narrative_blocks.header: She crossed party lines for FDR
narrative_blocks.body: Though the Oyster Bay Roosevelts were staunch Republicans, Belle campaigned for Franklin Roosevelt's reelection in 1944. Her father had owned Washington's Willard Hotel and served as ambassador to Spain.

cross_connections.display_label: also drawn by John Singer Sargent
another Willard who founded a pioneering school

notable.notable_blurb: CIA officer who ran the 1953 Iran coup

narrative_blocks.header: He ran the CIA's 1953 Iran coup
narrative_blocks.body: Operating under a cover name, Kim Roosevelt orchestrated Operation Ajax, the CIA-British plot that overthrew Iran's prime minister and restored the Shah in 1953. Decades later he broke Agency tradition by publishing a book on how it was done.

narrative_blocks.header: He refused to repeat the trick in Guatemala
narrative_blocks.body: Asked to engineer a second coup in Guatemala in 1954, Roosevelt declined, judging that Iran had worked only because so many Iranians shared its aims. He left the Agency a few years later for the oil and defense industries.

cross_connections.display_label: a Roosevelt cousin who ran CIA technical services
the general who ran Army intelligence in two wars
Washington's spymaster who ran the Culper Ring
a cousin and fellow CIA Arabist officer

bio.bio_blurb: CIA officer who ran the 1953 Iran coup

narrative_blocks.header: He taught a book group inside prisons
narrative_blocks.body: A Harvard-trained lawyer who once taught school in Tanzania, Jonathan found his calling in the Massachusetts prison system. For ten years he ran a revered book-discussion group for incarcerated men.

narrative_blocks.header: A Roosevelt who studied with Nadia Boulanger
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph trained as a pianist and composer, studying with the legendary Nadia Boulanger in France. He spent decades teaching music at the Hartt School, Mannes, and Longy.

narrative_blocks.header: He captained a warship supporting D-Day
narrative_blocks.body: Before his musical career Joseph commanded the destroyer transport USS Greene in the wartime Navy. His ship supported the Normandy landings.

narrative_blocks.header: He lectured on music at three universities
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond his own compositions, Joseph earned diplomas and degrees from several conservatories. He lectured on music at Columbia, Fairleigh Dickinson, and the New York College of Music.

cross_connections.display_label: the modernist poet, his first wife's father
his first wife Nancy's mother
a fellow descendant who led choirs and wrote on music
the Dial editor who first married Nancy's mother
the Irish senator who last married Nancy's mother
the model who became Nancy's father's companion
a descendant who composed Jingle Bells

bio.bio_blurb: Pianist-composer and Navy commander; Roosevelt grandson

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of a famous poet, married a Roosevelt
narrative_blocks.body: Nancy was the daughter of the poet E.E. Cummings, raised amid the literary world of Greenwich Village. She married Joseph Willard Roosevelt, and the marriage ended in divorce.

bio.bio_blurb: Second wife of Joseph Willard Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: Killed at nineteen, weeks into fatherhood
narrative_blocks.body: Simon died in a car accident at nineteen, only weeks after becoming a father. He left a wife and a ten-month-old child.

narrative_blocks.header: A Roosevelt killed driving his BMW at nineteen
narrative_blocks.body: Caleb, a student at Simon's Rock College, lost control of his car at an intersection and died of his injuries at nineteen. His passenger was critically hurt.

narrative_blocks.header: He died at twenty-one against a utility pole
narrative_blocks.body: David's pickup truck struck a utility pole in the Berkshires, killing him at twenty-one. He was the younger brother of Caleb, lost to a car crash four years before.

narrative_blocks.header: She campaigned for FDR against her own family
narrative_blocks.body: When the Oyster Bay Roosevelts backed Dewey in 1944, Clochette broke ranks and campaigned for her cousin Franklin's fourth term. She married John Gorham Palfrey, later dean of Columbia College.

notable.notable_blurb: Columbia dean and Atomic Energy commissioner

narrative_blocks.header: Dean of Columbia who policed the atom
narrative_blocks.body: Palfrey became dean of Columbia College at thirty-nine, then served on the Atomic Energy Commission under Kennedy and Johnson. A law professor, he pressed for control of the nuclear arms race.

narrative_blocks.header: He shared a Harvard class with John Kennedy
narrative_blocks.body: Palfrey graduated from Harvard in 1940 alongside the future President Kennedy. His five older sisters were all national junior tennis champions, one a two-time U.S. singles winner.

bio.bio_blurb: Columbia dean and Atomic Energy commissioner

narrative_blocks.header: A Boston pediatrician who led a Harvard house
narrative_blocks.body: Sean Palfrey practiced and taught pediatrics in Boston for decades. With his wife he served as faculty dean of Adams House at Harvard.

narrative_blocks.header: Poet and painter named for a Confederate spy
narrative_blocks.body: Antonia was a poet, photographer, and painter who took her name from her ancestress Antonia Ford, a Civil War Confederate agent. She made her life in the arts in western Massachusetts.

narrative_blocks.header: He drove ambulances for the British Eighth Army
narrative_blocks.body: Discharged from the Army for illness soon after enlisting at eighteen in 1943, he joined the American Field Service instead. He drove an ambulance with the British Eighth Army up through Italy until the fighting ended.

narrative_blocks.header: Oxford honors in history after the war
narrative_blocks.body: With the war over he read history at Oxford for three years and took his degree with high honors. He had prepared at Buckley, Groton, and Brooks before everything was interrupted.

narrative_blocks.header: Dead at twenty-eight in his Sutton Place bed
narrative_blocks.body: He was found dead one January morning in 1953 at his home on Sutton Place, the exact cause never established. He had lately come through surgery in England, where he was a postgraduate student at the University of London.

bio.bio_blurb: Kermit's son; Oxford historian and WWII volunteer

military_service.notes: Enlisted in the U.S. Army in 1943, discharged for illness, then drove an ambulance with the British Eighth Army in Italy.

notable.notable_blurb: 26th US President; Rough Rider; Nobel Peace laureate

cross_connections.display_label: his Harvard ornithology partner on an 1877 bird list
Trust-busted Morgan's Northern Securities Company in 1904
designed the townhouse his family used near the White House
drew up the guardianship papers for his children
the Columbia president and Nobel laureate he befriended

military_service.notes: Led the assault on the San Juan Heights, 1898; Medal of Honor awarded in 2001.

narrative_blocks.header: He married the childhood friend next door
narrative_blocks.body: Roosevelt married Edith Kermit Carow in London in 1886, his second wife and a granddaughter of the Union general Daniel Tyler. Through her he entered the Tyler family, with its Edwards and Hooker roots in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard ornithology friendship became his first publication
narrative_blocks.body: He met Henry Davis Minot, a Boston-based naturalist, as a Harvard freshman in 1877. They spent vacations listing birds in the Adirondacks and co-authored 'The Summer Birds of the Adirondacks' that fall — his first published work.

narrative_blocks.header: Trust-busted J.P. Morgan's Northern Securities Company
narrative_blocks.body: His administration brought the antitrust suit that dissolved Morgan's Northern Securities holding company in 1904, ending the merger of the Northern Pacific and Great Northern railroads. Morgan reportedly told him 'Send your man to my man and they can fix it up' — the President refused, and the Supreme Court sided with the government.

narrative_blocks.header: He befriended Columbia's president Nicholas Butler
narrative_blocks.body: Roosevelt's circle included Nicholas Murray Butler, the long-serving Columbia president and 1931 Nobel peace laureate, whom he nicknamed Nicholas Miraculous. The two Republican allies stayed close from Roosevelt's rise through the White House years.

narrative_blocks.header: The Roosevelts borrowed a famous architect's townhouse
narrative_blocks.body: When the White House was torn up for renovation in 1902, the family decamped to a townhouse belonging to the firm of Carrère and Hastings. Its partner Thomas Hastings would go on to design the New York Public Library and Arlington's Tomb of the Unknowns.

notable.notable_blurb: First Lady; wife of Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: She built the modern First Lady's office
narrative_blocks.body: Edith hired the first salaried social secretary for a First Lady and directed the 1902 White House renovation that created the West Wing. Historians credit her with shaping the modern institution of the role.

narrative_blocks.header: She burned a lifetime of love letters
narrative_blocks.body: After Theodore died, the intensely private Edith destroyed the letters he had written her across their courtship and marriage. It is part of why her inner life stayed, by her own design, almost unknowable.

narrative_blocks.header: She wrote her own Connecticut ancestors' history
narrative_blocks.body: Late in life Edith researched her mother's Tyler family and co-wrote a history of their colonial Connecticut roots. She bought the family's ancestral home in Brooklyn, Connecticut, returning there each summer.

narrative_blocks.header: She stumped for Hoover to disown Franklin
narrative_blocks.body: Edith kept out of politics for decades, then made a rare appearance for Herbert Hoover in 1932. Part of her motive was to remind voters that the Democratic nominee, Franklin Roosevelt, was only a distant cousin and not her son.

narrative_blocks.header: Theodore regretted overruling her judgment
narrative_blocks.body: A childhood neighbor whom Theodore Roosevelt married in 1886, Edith ran Sagamore Hill and raised six children while quietly advising him on people and politics. Roosevelt admitted he was worse off whenever he ignored her counsel.

cross_connections.display_label: was her stepdaughter, the capital’s sharpest wit
her husband President Roosevelt's father
her husband President Roosevelt's Hartford-born mother

bio.bio_blurb: First Lady; wife of Theodore Roosevelt

career.role: First Lady of the United States

notable.notable_blurb: Merchant-philanthropist; father of President Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: He helped found three great New York institutions
narrative_blocks.body: A glass-importing merchant and tireless philanthropist, Roosevelt helped establish the Metropolitan Museum of Art, the American Museum of Natural History, and the Children's Aid Society. He poured himself into charity for the city's poor children.

narrative_blocks.header: He sold Lincoln on a soldiers' pay plan
narrative_blocks.body: Devoted to the Union though his wife's sympathies ran Confederate, Roosevelt devised an allotment system letting soldiers send pay home to their families. He lobbied it through Washington with Lincoln's help and toured the Army of the Potomac signing men up.

cross_connections.display_label: the Hooker descendant his son President Roosevelt married

bio.bio_blurb: Merchant-philanthropist; father of President Theodore Roosevelt

notable.notable_blurb: Hartford-born mother of President Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: A Georgia belle who inspired Scarlett O'Hara
narrative_blocks.body: Mittie was a celebrated Southern beauty raised at Bulloch Hall in Roswell, Georgia, said to have partly inspired Margaret Mitchell's Scarlett O'Hara. Through the Civil War she remained an unreconstructed Confederate while her husband worked for the Union.

narrative_blocks.header: Two deaths in one house on Valentine's Day
narrative_blocks.body: On Saint Valentine's Day in 1884 Mittie died of typhoid in the family home as her son Theodore kept vigil. One floor above, his first wife was dying the same day, hours after bearing his daughter.

cross_connections.display_label: the Hooker descendant her son President Roosevelt married

bio.bio_blurb: Hartford-born mother of President Theodore Roosevelt

notable.notable_blurb: Shipowner; father of First Lady Edith Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: He owned the packet ship West Point
narrative_blocks.body: Carow inherited the Kermit and Carow shipping line from Robert Kermit, his father's partner, and owned the Liverpool packet West Point through the 1860s. The Civil War's collapse of American shipping ruined the trade and his fortunes declined.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter became a president's wife
narrative_blocks.body: The Carows lived beside the Roosevelts in New York, and his daughter Edith grew up alongside young Theodore. She married him in 1886 and became First Lady fifteen years later.

bio.bio_blurb: Shipowner; father of First Lady Edith Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: From Paris polish to genteel poverty
narrative_blocks.body: Educated in Paris during her father the general’s European years, Gertrude married the shipping heir Charles Carow in 1859 — and watched his drinking dissolve the family fortune. Widowed in 1883, she eventually moved to Italy, where living was cheaper.

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter grew up with the Roosevelts
narrative_blocks.body: The Carows were intimates of the East 20th Street Roosevelts, and her daughter Edith was raised in and out of Theodore’s nursery — becoming First Lady in 1901, six years after Gertrude died at Turin.

cross_connections.display_label: was the father of her husband Charles Carow
was the mother of her husband Charles Carow

bio.bio_blurb: Mother of First Lady Edith Roosevelt
